original_abstract: Precise targeting of bioactive helical peptides is essential for clinical diagnostics and therapeutics, yet de novo design of high-affinity binders for these flexible targets remains a significant challenge. While many peptide hormones, such as parathyroid hormone and glucagon, adopt alpha-helical structures upon receptor binding, they are often unstructured in isolation, making them difficult to target using traditional protein design methods. Here, we present a suite of computational strategies integrating parametric scaffold generation and deep learning—specifically RoseTTAFold (RFjoint) and RFdiffusion—to create potent peptide binders. By extending the RFdiffusion architecture to accommodate flexible targets and implementing a "partial diffusion" process for structural refinement, we generated binders with picomolar affinities—the highest reported for computationally designed proteins without experimental optimization. These de novo binders exhibit exceptional stability and specificity, outperforming natural receptors and matching antibody performance in diagnostic applications. We demonstrate their utility by constructing bioluminescence-based protein sensors and employing them as capture reagents for the sensitive detection of hormones in human serum via mass spectrometry. Our results highlight the power of generative deep learning to solve complex molecular recognition problems and provide a robust framework for developing the next generation of diagnostics for peptide biomarkers.
humanized_abstract: Designing peptides that accurately interact with bioactive helical proteins, including clinical diagnostics and therapeutics, is a significant challenge. In this study, we develop a set of computational tools leveraging parametric scaffold generation and deep learning, which includes RoseTTAFold (RFjoint) and an extension of the RoseTTAFold diffusion model (RFdiffusion), to design potent peptides that engage their target with picomolar affinity—the highest reported affinity of any computationally designed proteins lacking any information derived from experimental evolution. These peptides are stably folded and highly specific to their cognate protein receptors, comparable to that of antibodies. We validate their application in bioluminescent probes for protein sensors and as capture reagents for sensitive hormone detection in human serum using mass spectrometry. Our research exemplifies the capability of generative deep learning to address intricate molecular recognition problems and confirms that our technology platform is suitable for translating clinical diagnostics for peptide biomarkers into practice.

These peptides are stably folded and highly specific to their cognate protein receptors, comparable to that of antibodies. Our research exemplifies the capability of generative deep learning to address intricate molecular recognition problems and confirms that our technology platform is suitable for translating clinical diagnostics for peptide biomarkers into practice.
